reason: In this passage from Exodus 14 (the crossing of the Red Sea), the major decisions that drive the narrative are made by God and the leaders. God makes crucial decisions: hardening Pharaoh's heart (וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־ לֵב פַּרְעֹה), parting the sea (וַיּוֹלֶךְ יְהוָה אֶת־ הַיָּם), and defeating the Egyptian army. The human leaders also make key decisions: Pharaoh decides to pursue the Israelites (וַיֶּאְסֹר אֶת־ רִכְבּוֹ), and Moses is commanded by God to direct the people. In contrast, the common people of Israel express fear (וַיִּֽירְאוּ מְאֹד) and complain to Moses (וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל־ מֹשֶׁה), but they do not make decisions about events; rather, they are instructed by Moses to 'stand still and see the salvation of the Lord' (הִֽתְיַצְבוּ וּרְאוּ אֶת־ יְשׁוּעַת יְהוָה). The people respond passively to decisions made by their leader and God.
